subject: [PATCH 1/2] mm: zswap: free synchronous-IO writeback folios directly

reviews.author: Kairui Song
reviews.summary: Reviewer Kairui Song suggested renaming swap_cache_alloc_folio to __swap_cache_alloc_folio and updating its kdoc to inform callers that the folio will not be reclaimed if it is allocated off-LRU, and also asked whether adding a bool argument to skip_lru would improve the code.

2026-07-18.patch_summary: This patch optimizes the zswap writeback process by freeing synchronous-IO swap device folios directly after writing, rather than leaving them on the LRU for page reclaim to handle later. This reduces memory residency time and avoids unnecessary reclaim scans. The change is specific to synchronous-IO devices, as asynchronous and filesystem-backed swaps complete in interrupt context and are handled separately.

reviews.author: Nhat Pham
reviews.summary: The reviewer suggested adding a check to ensure the folio is not dirty or swapped out before freeing it, citing potential issues with racing swapin operations and incorrect behavior in certain edge cases.
reviews.raw_body:
Hmm I think you also have to test for writeback and dirty here right?

After a racing swapin freed the swap slot, the folio might be dirtied
and swapped out again. We probably shouldn't try to remove_mapping()
such a folio?

We also do similar check for zswap/sync-io devices in pageout handling
(mm/vmscan.c, shrink_folio_list()), and the other dropbehind handler
(filemap_end_dropbehind()).

When I was playing with this, I also tested the folio with
folio_test_active() and folio_mapped(), but I must admit - I do not
remember why I did it. Could just be me making sure we dont get rid of
frequently used folio rather than for strict correctness. :)

2026-07-20.patch_summary: This patch optimizes the zswap writeback process by freeing synchronous-IO swap device folios directly after writing, rather than leaving them on the LRU for page reclaim to handle later. This reduces memory residency time and avoids unnecessary reclaim scans. The change is specific to synchronous-IO devices, as asynchronous and filesystem-backed swaps complete in interrupt context and are handled separately.

reviews.author: Johannes Weiner
reviews.summary: Reviewer Johannes Weiner questioned whether this change is specific to zswap or could be applied more broadly to the generic swapout path, suggesting that the function and dropbehind queue could be reused.
reviews.raw_body:
Is this actually zswap-specific or should it be just swap code?

Both this function and the dropbehind queue in the next patch seem
like they could be reused if we wanted to make the generic swapout
path do dropbehind as well.

It looks odd in the next patch to have a generic swapcache &&
dropbehind check and then call zswap code.

reviews.author: Alexandre Ghiti
reviews.summary:
Reviewer noted that the patch does not handle the case where a swapin has claimed the folio before it is freed, and requested additional checks to ensure the folio is not reclaimed prematurely.

Reviewer Alexandre Ghiti noted that the writeback check in the patch is not necessary because dropbehind folios are allocated off LRU, making it impossible for a racing swapin to reclaim and swap out the folio while under writeback. However, he also suggested adding the check as a precautionary measure, considering other potential 'swap triggers' like MADV_PAGEOUT.

Reviewer Alexandre Ghiti pointed out that folio_mapped() is unnecessary because remove_mapping() checks the refcount, and also noted that folio_test_active() will always be false when allocated off-lru, requesting confirmation before checking for it in writeback paths.
reviews.raw_body:
Hi Nhat,

On Mon, Jul 20, 2026 at 5:56 PM Nhat Pham <nphamcs@gmail.com> wrote:

---

Actually it took me some time to eliminate the writeback check, I
think it is not necessary.

First, we only call remove_mapping() at the end of writeback (easy scenario).

Then comes your scenario, the hard one. But it cannot happen because
dropbehind folios are allocated off lru. Which means that a racing
swapin can re-dirty the folio (that will get caught by remove_mapping
itself) but it cannot be swapped out (ie under writeback) because it
is off lru and then cannot be reclaimed.

But writing that, I realize I may have missed other "swap triggers"
(MADV_PAGEOUT for example). Perhaps adding the check is safer (and
cheap anyway), what do you think?

---

folio_mapped() is not necessary because remove_mapping() checks the
refcount. folio_test_active() is always false in my case since
allocated off-lru.

So unless someone confirms what I said above for writeback, I'll check
for it, it's cheap anyway.

Thanks,

Alex

reviews.author: Alexandre Ghiti
reviews.summary:
Reviewer noted that the patch does not handle asynchronous and filesystem-backed swap devices correctly, as they complete writeback in interrupt context where the folio cannot be freed.

Reviewer Alexandre Ghiti agreed that freeing synchronous-IO writeback folios directly is correct, but requested to revisit the patch after gathering statistics on generic swap folios.
sentiment_signals: async and fs-backed swap devices not handled
reviews.raw_body:
Hi Johannes,

On Tue, Jul 21, 2026 at 1:47 PM Johannes Weiner <hannes@cmpxchg.org> wrote:

---

Yes, agreed, Yosry made the same comment!

I'll come back with a new version when I have numbers for generic swap folios.

Thanks,

Alex

2026-07-21.patch_summary: This patch optimizes the zswap writeback process by freeing synchronous-IO swap device folios directly after writing, rather than leaving them on the LRU for page reclaim to handle later. This reduces memory residency time and avoids unnecessary reclaim scans. The change is specific to synchronous-IO devices, as asynchronous and filesystem-backed swaps complete in interrupt context and are handled separately.

reviews.author: Barry Song
reviews.summary:
Reviewer Barry Song noted that the patch does not provide an equivalent mechanism to reclaim folios directly like MGLRU does, and requested a similar approach for synchronous-IO writeback

Reviewer Barry Song noted that asynchronous swap devices do not free memory immediately and suggested freeing anonymous folios directly after writeout to avoid occupying memory until another reclaim scan

Reviewer noted that the patch does not handle the case where a concurrent swapin has claimed the folio, and requested that the lock be dropped before calling try_to_unmap()
reviews.raw_body:
With MGLRU, we already have code like the one below that reclaims
folios directly without requiring another reclaim scan, whereas the
active/inactive LRU path doesn't have an equivalent mechanism.

                /* retry folios that may have missed
folio_rotate_reclaimable() */
                if (!skip_retry && !folio_test_active(folio) &&
!folio_mapped(folio) &&
                    !folio_test_dirty(folio) && !folio_test_writeback(folio)) {
                        list_move(&folio->lru, &clean);
                        continue;
                }

---

Might be a bit off-topic, but I've been thinking about this problem
for a while.

MADV_PAGEOUT, and reclaim in general, don't actually free memory
when using asynchronous swap devices. Do you think it would make
sense to free the folio directly, at least for anonymous folios,
once it has been written out, instead of waiting for another reclaim
scan?

It seems a bit odd that after MADV_PAGEOUT completes, the memory is
still occupied.

---

Best Regards
Barry

reviews.author: Kairui Song
reviews.summary: Reviewer Kairui Song suggested limiting the immediate drop of swap cache folios to specific cases like MADV_PAGEOUT and zswap cold writeback, as it can help catch fast refaults for common swap, but is not necessary for direct synchronous IO devices or zswap compression.
reviews.raw_body:
Yes but I think that immediate drop might be actually better be
limited to things like MADV_PAGEOUT and zswap cold writeback? For
common swap, keeping the swap cache after writeback sometimes helps a
lot by catching fast refaults. Because we perform swap writeback
asynchronously, we can easily overshoot the reclaim and send more
folios into writeback than necessary.

For direct synchronous IO devices or direct zswap compression that's
not a problem because their writeback is synchronized, so the
reclaimer waits. And we already free them immediately after writeback.

2026-07-22.patch_summary: This patch optimizes the zswap writeback process by freeing synchronous-IO swap device folios directly after writing, rather than leaving them on the LRU for page reclaim to handle later. This reduces memory residency time and avoids unnecessary reclaim scans. The change is specific to synchronous-IO devices, as asynchronous and filesystem-backed swaps complete in interrupt context and are handled separately.

reviews.author: Johannes Weiner
reviews.summary: The reviewer noted that the patch does not store an eviction shadow like the reclaim path, which means no refaults are detected from dropbehind pages, potentially altering reclaim decisions, zswap shrinker force, and pressure detection.
reviews.raw_body:
I just chatted with Alexandre off-list, but this path does not store
an eviction shadow like the reclaim path does. That means no refaults
are detected from dropbehind pages, which can fundamentally alter
reclaim decisions, zswap shrinker force, and pressure detection.

reviews.author: Nhat Pham
reviews.summary: Reviewer Nhat Pham identified a potential zswap shrinker bug in the original patch, pointing out that when allocating a new buffer for writeback and adding it to swap cache, the old shadow is erased. He suggested saving the existing shadow and handing it over to zswap, which would require juggling shadows in various paths, including swapin, remove_mapping, and zswap operations.
reviews.raw_body:
I think it's a zswap shrinker bug even before this patch. When we
allocate a new buffer for the writeback request and add to swap cache,
we erase the old shadow :(

I had a patch sometimes ago to fix this - basically, what it did is
when we allocate the buffer for writeback and add it to swap cache, we
save the existing shadow and hand that shadow to zswap. zswap will
store it in the zswap tree tree (in place of the struct zswap entry
that is being freed).

Now you will have to juggle it a bit in various path:

1. swapin has to check the zswap tree for refault.

2. remove_mapping does not create a new shadow, but instead restore
the zswap's owned shadow, if there is one.

3. zswap operations have to now handle shadows stored in its trees too.

I think there is one edge case where we still lose the old shadow (I
need to check), but the rest seems to work.

It's a bit complex, yes, but I managed to find more uses for this
added complexity too. With this, I was able to add more monitoring to
zswap shrinker - you can track how many shadows are being stored in
the zswap tree, which gives you a per-cgroup counter of inflight zswap
buffers. This helps with diagnosing when zswap writeback is generating
too much memory pressure with these buffers. You can also use this
counter it for zswap writeback heuristics (for instance, throttle
writeback when there are too many inflight buffers already and we're
not freeing them fast enough).

I have not had the chance to make it upstream-ready, given all of my
attention being taken away by vswap and other endeavors, and the fact
that swap architecture has changed quite a bit.

reviews.author: Nhat Pham
reviews.summary: Reviewer Nhat Pham noted that freeing synchronous-IO writeback folios directly can lead to inaccurate workingset tracking heuristics, as it incorrectly reports refaults on cold pages, and requested consideration of this issue.
reviews.raw_body:
... and count it as a refault :| Which is even worse than just silent
erasure. It throws off all our workingset tracking heuristics,
inaccurately reporting refault on a really cold page (writeback
candidate).

2026-07-23.patch_summary: This patch optimizes the zswap writeback process by freeing synchronous-IO swap device folios directly after writing, rather than leaving them on the LRU for page reclaim to handle later. This reduces memory residency time and avoids unnecessary reclaim scans. The change is specific to synchronous-IO devices, as asynchronous and filesystem-backed swaps complete in interrupt context and are handled separately.
